summary: This very brief post links to Joel Spolsky's Inc. magazine column about recognizing employee contributions. The core idea is that visible, direct contributions to revenue don't necessarily represent more value than indirect contributions from other team members. Spolsky challenges the common tendency to disproportionately reward or credit the person whose work most visibly translates into money. The post argues for a more equitable understanding of how value is created in teams.
key_points: Visible, direct contributions to revenue can overshadow equally valuable indirect contributions from other team members
Individual programmer ideas that generate money don't diminish the value added by the rest of the team
Fair recognition requires looking beyond direct revenue attribution
money_quotes: Simply because one programmer's idea translated visibly and directly into a lot of money didn't mean that the other team members weren't adding just as much value to the business, albeit in a less direct way.
key_insight: The visibility of a contribution's impact on revenue is not a reliable measure of its actual value to the business.
